packs.title: Everyday + trail basics
packs.description: Year-round judgment, neighborhood heat, cars, other dogs, and trail habits. Works when you are not hiking.
packs.regionNote: General guidance; not veterinary or legal advice. Verify local rules yourself.

packs.title: Tricks & skills
packs.description: Teach cues in short sessions: targeting, spins, settle, paw, and clean mechanics. For serious behavior problems, use a professional.
packs.regionNote: Educational only. For aggression, fear, or bite risk, consult a qualified trainer or behavior consultant.

packs.title: NorCal seasonal notes
packs.description: Fog layers, inland heat gaps, winter mud, and coastal humidity patterns.
packs.regionNote: Framed for Northern California microclimates; not exhaustive.

cards.title: Heat stress: what to watch for
cards.subtitle: Earlier is better, turn back before collapse risk.
body: Excessive panting that does not ease with a short rest in shade.
Bright red gums, thick saliva, or reluctance to move.
Wobbling, vomiting, or diarrhea on the trail.
Your dog lies down and will not pick up pace again after water.
When in doubt, shorten the outing, cool gradually (not ice shock), and contact a veterinarian if signs persist or worsen.
cards.disclaimer: Educational only. For health concerns, call your veterinarian.

cards.title: How much water to carry
cards.subtitle: A simple planning frame, not a formula.
body: Start with what you used last time on a similar route, temperature, and elevation gain.
Hot days and sun exposure increase needs; add capacity before you need it.
Carry a collapsible bowl or train your dog to drink from a bottle stream you control.
Creeks are unreliable; treat seasonal water as a bonus, not the plan.
If your dog is slowing, panting harder than usual, or seeking shade, that is often a hydration or heat signal.

cards.title: Paw care: heat, ice, and grit
cards.subtitle: Protect feet before problems show up.
body: Hot pavement and rock radiate heat; early morning or shaded routes reduce risk.
Snow crust and ice balls between pads are common; check paws at transitions.
Booties help on abrasive volcanic rock or long gravel; practice at home first.
Trim fur between pads carefully to reduce ice buildup; avoid cutting skin.
After the hike, rinse paws if deicers or harsh dust were present.

cards.title: Recall and leash: a mindset frame
cards.subtitle: Leash laws vary; your dog does not read signs.
body: Strong recall is a skill, not a vibe, proof it with distance, distraction, and fatigue.
If your dog is new, reactive, or wildlife is active, default to leash even where off-leash is common.
Yield to others: step off trail, shorten leash, and give space when passing.
Carry high-value treats and practice stops mid-hike so ‘come’ works when it matters.
When uncertain, choose the option that reduces conflict with people, wildlife, and other dogs.
Always check official land-manager rules for the trail you are on.
cards.disclaimer: Not legal advice. Follow posted regulations.

cards.title: Wildlife buffer basics
cards.subtitle: Distance protects everyone.
body: If you see predators or large ungulates, create space calmly, no chasing for photos.
Pick up small dogs if a coyote is curious and you are in range; move away confidently.
Rattlesnakes: give width, keep dog controlled, and learn your regional protocol with your vet.
Rodenticides and bait stations near trailheads can poison dogs, scan edges and keep dogs close.
Report aggressive wildlife encounters per local guidance.
cards.disclaimer: Educational only. Regional laws and risks differ.

cards.title: Trail first-aid: small kit ideas
cards.subtitle: Carry what you know how to use.
body: Vet wrap, gauze, tweezers, saline rinse, tick tool (if ticks are local), antiseptic wipes.
A backup leash and a muzzle that allows panting if your dog is injured and scared.
Phone offline maps and the number for the nearest emergency vet along your route.
Benadryl or other meds only if your veterinarian already gave you dosing guidance.
Serious bleeding, non-weight-bearing lameness, heat collapse suspected → get professional help.

cards.title: Simple turn-back rules
cards.subtitle: Ego loses to weather.
body: If conditions are worse than you planned, shorten the loop early, miles are optional.
If your dog’s attitude shifts (lagging, stress sniffing, constant stopping), listen.
If water is running low relative to remaining sun exposure, retreat.
Lightning, smoke, or rapid temperature swing: prioritize exit over summits.
A good day is one where you both come home capable of doing it again soon.

cards.title: 60-second post-hike check
cards.subtitle: Ticks, burrs, pads, and attitude.
body: Scan ears, armpits, groin, and between toes for ticks and foxtails (regional).
Feel each pad for cuts, look for swelling, note limp that shows up after rest.
Offer water and a meal after cooling, not during heavy panting.
Note anything unusual in your trail log so next time you spot trends.
If something worries you, photograph it and message your vet clinic for triage.

cards.title: Elevation gain: pacing
cards.subtitle: Steep is different for four legs.
body: More vertical means more heat load and joint load; add breaks on south-facing segments.
Descents can be harder on shoulders and pads than climbs, control speed.
If your dog is new to big days, build volume across weeks, not one huge jump.
Carry calories appropriate to effort if your vet recommends snacks mid-hike.
Altitude sickness in dogs is uncommon but possible, lethargy, cough, or distress merit stopping.

cards.title: Low light and visibility
cards.subtitle: Be boring to drivers and predictable on trail.
body: Reflective leash tabs, collar lights, or small blinkers help at dusk trailheads.
If you road-walk to the trail, assume cars do not see you.
Headlamps: angle down so you do not blind other hikers or dogs.
Coyote and cat activity often rises at dawn and dusk, adjust control accordingly.

cards.title: Hot neighborhood walks
cards.subtitle: Asphalt and timing, not only “mountain heat.”
body: Pavement and dark asphalt heat up faster than grass; early walks and shaded routes beat midday loops when it is warm.
If you cannot hold the back of your hand on pavement comfortably for several seconds, assume it is rough on paws, shorten, use booties, or reroute to cooler ground.
Flat walks still stack heat: carry water, stop in shade, and watch pant quality, not only distance.
Short-nosed breeds, seniors, and heavy-coated dogs often hit limits sooner, bias shorter and earlier.
If your dog plants, lags, or panting ramps up without settling, treat it as a heat signal, head home and cool gradually.
cards.disclaimer: Educational only. Urgent or severe signs call your veterinarian.

cards.title: Dog + car: trip framing
cards.subtitle: Errands, road days, and why “quick” is still a plan.
body: Parked vehicles heat dangerously fast on warm days, treat leaving a dog in a sun-baked car as a last resort, not a default.
Errands: dog-home vs dog-along is a real decision, combine trips, use curbside pickup, or tag-team with another adult when heat is in play.
Moving vehicles: loose dogs in cabins are projectiles; crates and crash-tested restraints are different tools, research what matches your vehicle and dog with qualified guidance.
Road trips: water, potty, and stretch rhythms beat marathon driving; stress is still workload for your dog.
Truck bed travel where legal still demands restraint, weather cover, and pad protection, wind and road debris are hazards, not accessories.
cards.disclaimer: Educational only. Follow local laws and manufacturer guidance.

cards.title: Another dog is coming (you are on leash)
cards.subtitle: Space first. Calm exits beat heroics.
body: If you cannot read the oncoming dog or handler, bias space, cross the street, loop wide, or pause behind a parked car instead of tight passes.
Short leash, minimal slack, no forced greetings; most scuffles start from surprise proximity.
Ask before sniff hellos; not every leashed dog wants contact, and “friendly” is a claim, not a guarantee.
If you need separation and the other walker is drifting in, be boring and clear: “We need space”, then move decisively.
If contact turns into a fight, protect people first, then separate without grabbing collars blindly; trade info calmly afterward and involve your vet for bite assessment.
cards.disclaimer: Educational only. For serious reactivity, work with a qualified professional.

cards.title: Coastal fog vs inland heat
cards.subtitle: The same day can feel like two seasons.
body: Marine layer can keep coast cool while 20 miles inland is aggressively hot by noon.
If you stage near the coast and drive inland, pack for the hotter forecast, not the parking lot temperature.
Fog moisture can chill dogs that are wet and stopped, bring a light layer for long breaks on windy ridges.
Visibility drops on roads, leash near trailheads and parking lots.
Check multiple forecast points along your route, not only your home zip code.

cards.title: Smoke and air quality days
cards.subtitle: Rest days are training too.
body: Dogs filter particulates less efficiently than humans assume; prioritize AQI and official advisories.
If you smell smoke or see haze, shorten intensity and choose lower valleys if air is cleaner there.
Brachycephalic breeds and older dogs are higher risk, bias to canceling hard efforts.
Watch for eye irritation, cough, or unusual fatigue after outings.
Sometimes the win is a sniff walk on a cleaner day instead of grinding miles.
cards.disclaimer: Educational only. Follow public health guidance.

cards.title: Winter mud and trail ethics
cards.subtitle: Don’t widen the trail trying to dodge puddles.
body: Deep mud can stress tendons and hide holes, slow down and pick firm lines.
If a trail is sodden, consider an alternate route to reduce erosion.
Rinse paws after mud that might hide sharp grit or oak irritants (regional).
Hypother risk is usually about combo of wet + wind + stop time, manage breaks.
If the land manager posts seasonal closures, treat them as non-optional.

cards.title: Grass season and stickers
cards.subtitle: Regional nuisances change month to month.
body: Long grass corridors can mean ticks or burrs depending on area, post-hike checks matter.
Short coats are easier to inspect; long coats may need combing at trailhead.
Booties can help on cheatgrass-heavy stretches if your dog tolerates them.
If your vet recommends preventatives, timing matters, ask during seasonal transitions.
Eye squinting or pawing at face after a hike can mean seed or irritation, escalate quickly.

cards.title: Granite and abrasive rock
cards.subtitle: Sierra-style footing quirks.
body: Rough granite can wear pads on long days, condition gradually and consider booties for sharp sections.
Water crossings can make rock slick, test footing before committing your dog.
Snow cups on paws return in shoulder season, carry trim tool you know how to use safely.
Midday sun on light rock radiates heat, adjust start time before peak temperatures.
Carry enough water, shade is patchy on exposed ridges.

cards.title: Canopy cool, still planning hot
cards.subtitle: Shade is not the same as safety on long climbs.
body: Closed canopy feels cooler but humidity can reduce evaporative cooling from panting.
Still carry water for both of you; assume exit segments may be sunny.
Slippery duff and roots are common, shorten leash on steep stairs.
Cold creeks feel great; sudden big chills for wet small dogs can be rough, dry and move.
Poison oak proximity matters for dogs that brush coat, learn local lookalikes.

cards.title: Delta breeze timing
cards.subtitle: Afternoon windows shift risk.
body: In parts of the Bay Area, late afternoon wind can help, or it can sandblast a ridgeline.
Plan water stops before the hottest segment, not after you are already behind.
If you commute to the trail, your dog may be warmer in the car than on foot, never leave dogs in parked vehicles.
Evening outings can be great; carry lights and watch for changing wildlife behavior.
Heat radiates from urban pavement near trailheads, minimize standing on hot asphalt.

cards.title: How tricks sessions work
cards.subtitle: Reward what you like, make the right choice easy.
body: Train when your dog is rested enough to think, not right after wild play unless you are practicing calm-down.
Use tiny, rapid treats and a clear marker (word ‘yes’ or a click) the instant the behavior happens.
One main skill per session keeps you both from drifting; five good minutes beat thirty confused ones.
If your dog checks out, make it easier (smaller motion, closer target, better treats), do not drill through frustration.
Fade food lures into hand signals and verbal cues only after the motion is reliable.
cards.disclaimer: Educational only. Not a replacement for professional help with fear or aggression.

cards.title: Hand target (touch)
cards.subtitle: Nose to palm, portable superpower.
body: Present an open palm an inch from your dog’s nose; when the nose bumps, mark and treat.
Reset by pulling hand behind your back, then offer again like a fresh rep.
Add one inch of distance only after five crisp touches in a row at the current distance.
Name it (‘touch’) once the motion is snappy; say the word just before the hand appears.
Use it to move your dog around you, onto curbs, away from dropped food, or into heel position.

cards.title: Spin / twirl
cards.subtitle: Tight circles, two directions if you can.
body: Lure nose with treat in a small circle beside the dog’s shoulder; mark when the front feet finish the turn.
Keep circles big enough that your dog is not twisting the spine harshly; slippery floors need caution.
Teach one direction to fluency before mirroring; name them differently (‘spin’ vs ‘twist’) if you use both.
Stop if your dog shows stiffness, hopping, or reluctance; some bodies do not love repeated tight spins.
Alternate with forward walking or tricks that lengthen the spine so sessions stay balanced.
cards.disclaimer: Educational only. Pause if you see discomfort or joint concerns; ask your vet for seniors.

cards.title: Mat or bed (‘place’)
cards.subtitle: Go to a spot, lie down, chill.
body: Drop a mat; mark any paw on it at first, then only full four-on or a down if that is your goal.
Deliver treats on the mat so the bed predicts good things happen there, not only at your pocket.
Add a release cue (‘okay’) before you invite them off so ‘place’ means stay until freed.
Increase duration one second at a time; if they break, make the goal easier next rep.
Carry a thin travel mat for patios, gates, or vet lobbies once the pattern is solid at home.

cards.title: Paw / shake
cards.subtitle: Voluntary lift, not a grabbed foot.
body: Hide a treat in a closed fist low; when your dog paws at it, mark and treat the paw lift.
Open criteria slowly: wait for a gentle paw instead of scraping or mouthing.
Add your outstretched hand cue only after the behavior is offered without the fist bait every time.
Reset between reps so you are not drilling the same frantic pawing.
If your dog is shy about paws, build trust with slow handling games outside this trick first.

cards.title: Leave it (starter frame)
cards.subtitle: Impulse control before street food or wildlife.
body: Start with a boring treat in your closed hand; mark when your dog stops fussing at it for a breath.
Reward from the other hand so ‘leave that’ does not mean ‘steal it on release’.
Progress to treat on floor under your foot, then exposed with you ready to cover if they dive.
Add distance and real-world temptations only after high success indoors on a leash.
‘Leave it’ is a cue, not a moral lecture; make the alternative (check in with you) reliably better.
cards.disclaimer: Educational only. Real-world poison or traps still need distance and management.

cards.title: Fading the food lure
cards.subtitle: So cues work without a magnet in your fingers.
body: After ten crisp reps with treat at the dog’s nose, try the same motion with an empty hand then feed from pocket.
If the dog freezes, insert one more lured rep, then try empty-hand again at easier angle.
Put the food away between reps so the pattern is behavior → marker → reward, not treat visible 100% of the time.
Name the cue only after the empty-hand signal works most tries.
Keep jackpot treats for new locations or harder distractions, not every rep forever.

cards.title: Sit
cards.subtitle: Default “park the dog” behavior.
body: Lure nose up and slightly back so the haunches drop; mark the moment butt hits floor, then treat at chest height.
If they pop back up, feed only while seated; standing gets silence, sitting gets the reward sequence.
Add the word ‘sit’ only when they sit five times in a row from your hand signal alone.
Practice on different surfaces and after mild excitement, not only in the quiet kitchen.
For giant or stiff dogs, lower criteria or ask your vet about joint comfort before lots of repeated sits.

cards.title: Down / lie
cards.subtitle: Elbows on the ground, body long.
body: From sit, lure nose straight down to the floor between the paws, then forward along the ground like an L.
If they crawl, lure under a low hand or knee to block the scoot until elbows settle.
Mark the flat moment; deliver treat on the floor between the paws so down stays geometrically true.
Release with ‘okay’ before you invite them up so ‘down’ means stay down until freed.
Some dogs hate belly exposure; build on carpet first and never force the position.

cards.title: Stay (starter)
cards.subtitle: Duration without drifting toward the cookie.
body: Choose sit or down; step back half a step; return and feed in position before they break.
Return to the dog to pay the treat early; don’t call them to you until duration is boring at one step.
Add a clear release cue; if they leave before it, quietly reset without a jackpot.
Grow time in seconds, not minutes; three-second stays that hold beat thirty-second guesses.
Real world: add leash and doorway distance only after solid calm reps at home.
cards.disclaimer: Educational only. ‘Stay’ near traffic or cliffs still needs physical barriers and leashes.

cards.title: Middle (between legs)
cards.subtitle: Standing hideout and crowd buffer.
body: Stand with feet shoulder-width; lure the dog forward through from behind until they are centered.
Mark when shoulders align with yours; feed in position so staying between pays.
Add one slow step forward only after they can hold still for a few seconds.
Widen stance for long dogs; this should feel roomy, not trapped.
Skip if your dog freezes or panics in tight spaces; build confidence with mat work first.

cards.title: Play bow
cards.subtitle: Front down, butt up — great stretch cue.
body: From standing, lure nose down to the floor between the front paws while the rear stays as high as comfortable.
Mark the elongated shape before the dog flops to a full down.
Keep sessions on non-slip flooring; bow on ice is a hard no.
Name it (‘bow’ or ‘take a bow’) once the shape is predictable from an empty-hand lure.
Pairs well as a reset between high-arousal tricks so the dog learns to drop front end without quitting the game.

cards.title: Back up
cards.subtitle: Reverse steps on cue.
body: Face your dog; take a slow step toward them; mark any rearward shuffle before the chest bonks you.
Reward with toss behind them or feed at your shin so backward motion stays the game.
Fade the forward lean into a palm-toward-chest signal or verbal ‘back’ once steps are fluent.
Clear the path so they do not trip on curbs, walls, or other dogs.
Elder dogs or those with hind-end weakness: keep reps few and surfaces friendly; ask your vet if unsure.

cards.title: Peekaboo (through legs, front)
cards.subtitle: Dog sits facing you under your straddle.
body: Stand with feet apart; lure the dog to thread forward until their head rises between your legs and they face you.
Mark when the head is centered and they offer attention upward; treat from above between your knees.
Widen your stance if the dog feels crowded; mind slick floors and bag straps.
Add duration only after they happily hold the slot without pawing your legs.
If jumping replaces sitting, reset to fewer steps and reward calmer approach only.

checklists.title: 60-second trail readiness
checklists.description: Quick screen before you clip the leash.

items.label: I checked the forecast at the trail elevation, not just at home.
items.hint: Inland vs coastal can differ sharply.

items.label: I have enough water for the full outing plus a margin.
items.hint: Treat natural water as extra, not guaranteed.

items.label: Paws and fitness match today’s surface and vert.
items.hint: New rock or big vert needs pacing.

items.label: Recall/leash plan matches wildlife season and trail busyness.
I know today’s turn-back triggers (heat, water, attitude).
Earlier start or shadier route selected.
Planned rest shade every 20–40 minutes depending on effort.
I can shorten the route without drama.
Post-hike cool-down space at home or car is sorted.
items.hint: Not trapped in a hot vehicle.

items.label: I checked recent trip reports for depth and mell-out timing.
Pad care: ice balls trimmed or booties ready.
Extra calories and breaks accounted for.
If Postholing for me, it is rough for my dog, reassess.

checklists.title: After-hike quick log prompts
checklists.description: Build memory without turning it into homework.

items.label: Anything new with paws, limp, or stool within 24h?
Water consumed vs usual, more or less?
Heat or mood stress moments, where on the route?
items.hint: Change timing or shade next visit.

checklists.title: 10-minute trick session
checklists.description: Short, happy reps. End before anyone gets fried.

items.label: Quiet space, dog fed enough to think but not stuffed.
Pick one trick or one step of a trick only.
Marker timing: bridge the exact moment, then deliver treat.
8–15 clean reps or stop sooner if focus fades.
Note what to tighten next time (distance, distraction, cue).

hazardCalendar.title: January, mud and early dark
bullets: Short daylight → lights and visibility planning.
Saturated trails → erosion ethics; pick firm surfaces.
Cold wet combos → manage stop time and insulation after water crossings.

hazardCalendar.title: February, winter holds, sneaky heat inland
bullets: Inland valley preview days can spike, read multi-point forecasts.
Ticks may wake early in mild winters, keep check habit.
Snow trips → carry paw/ball ice management tools you have practiced with.

hazardCalendar.title: March, grass rises, pollen and burrs
bullets: Long grass corridors → post-hike combing and tick checks where relevant.
Allergies can mirror irritation, ask your vet if eyes/paws flare.
Fast-changing creek crossings, test depth and current.

hazardCalendar.title: April, spring crowds and snakes waking
bullets: More users → leash courtesy and passing drills.
Snake awareness returns in many regions, control and sight lines.
Baby wildlife season in many areas, extra distance defaults.

hazardCalendar.title: May, real heat rehearsals
bullets: Afternoon inland temps climb, bias morning starts.
Radiating rock and road heat near trailheads.
Carry extra water before ‘we might need it’ becomes true.

hazardCalendar.title: June, early summer discipline
bullets: Smoke can appear depending on year, check AQI habits.
Water sources dry earlier at low elevations, plan carries.
Ticks and fleas peak in many zones, stay consistent with vet plan.

hazardCalendar.title: July, peak heat strategy
bullets: Schedule around sun, not only around your calendar.
Brachycephalic and older dogs → conservative defaults.
Water crossing hype, giardia risk is regional and seasonal; ask your vet about exposure.

hazardCalendar.title: August, smoke and ozone weeks
bullets: Cancel intensity on bad air days; low valleys vs ridges can differ.
Eye and airway irritation → stop early.
Wildlife stress in dry years, more nighttime movement possible.

hazardCalendar.title: September, hottest tail risk
bullets: Inland heat can extend, do not autopilot ‘fall is cool’.
Rattlesnake encounters still common in warm zones.
Shorter water carries still fail fast if you overshoot shade.

hazardCalendar.title: October, best miles, variable nights
bullets: Daytime perfection can hide early sunset chill, layer for stops.
Hunting seasons in many public lands, visibility gear and control.
Acorns / dropped fruit in some parks, ingestion risks vary by dog.

hazardCalendar.title: November, rain returns, traction changes
bullets: Slick wood bridges and mud camber, shorten line on steep bits.
Creek crossings swell, do not assume summer lines.
Dark commutes near trailheads, leash discipline.

hazardCalendar.title: December, short days, holiday crowding
bullets: Parking chaos → dog stress spikes; keep exit paths clean.
Cold rain vs snow depends on elevation, pack for the parking lot lie.
Gift-noise holidays → some dogs need space from crowds.
